q: What crops grow in Niger State?
a: Niger State is best known for maize, rice, yam, cassava, soybean, and groundnut. Its Guinea Savanna climate with 1000-1500mm rainfall supports a wide variety of grains and tubers.

q: What crops grow in Kano State?
a: Kano State is best for maize, sorghum, millet, cowpea, groundnut, onion, and tomato. It sits in the Sudan Savanna zone and is also Nigeria's largest grain trading hub via Dawanau Market.

q: What crops grow in Kaduna State?
a: Kaduna State grows maize, sorghum, ginger, tomato, onion, and irish potato (in the southern highlands). It has a Guinea Savanna to Sudan Savanna climate suited to both grains and cash crops.

q: What crops grow in Benue State?
a: Benue State, known as the Food Basket of the Nation, is best for yam, soybean, rice, cassava, sesame, and citrus. It has fertile loamy soil across the Guinea Savanna zone.

q: What crops grow in Plateau State?
a: Plateau State's cool highland climate makes it Nigeria's main producer of irish potato, carrot, cabbage, lettuce, strawberry, and temperate vegetables. It is also good for maize and acha (fonio).

q: What crops grow in Oyo State?
a: Oyo State grows cassava, maize, yam, cashew, cocoa, and vegetables. It sits in the derived savanna zone with two rainy seasons per year.

q: What crops grow in Ondo State?
a: Ondo State is Nigeria's top cocoa-producing state, also strong in oil palm, cassava, plantain, and rubber. Its rainforest climate has abundant year-round rainfall.

q: What crops grow in Cross River State?
a: Cross River State is strong in cocoa, cassava, plantain, oil palm, pineapple, and rubber, supported by heavy rainforest rainfall of 1500-4000mm annually.

q: What crops grow in Kebbi State?
a: Kebbi State is Nigeria's leading rice-producing state, especially around Argungu and Birnin Kebbi, and also grows onion, wheat, and millet under irrigation from the Sokoto-Rima river system.

q: What crops grow in Sokoto State?
a: Sokoto State grows onion, wheat, millet, cowpea, and sorghum. It sits in the Sahel to Sudan Savanna zone with a long dry season and heavy reliance on irrigation.

q: What crops grow in Taraba State?
a: Taraba State, including the cool Mambilla Plateau, grows tea, coffee, irish potato, ginger, maize, and yam. It has one of the most varied climates in Nigeria from lowland to highland.

q: What crops grow in Nasarawa State?
a: Nasarawa State is a major producer of ginger, sesame, soybean, yam, and maize, benefiting from fertile Guinea Savanna soils.

q: What crops grow in Anambra State?
a: Anambra State grows cassava, rice, yam, and vegetables, with the Anambra rice basin being one of the important rice-growing floodplains in the southeast.

q: What crops grow in Delta State?
a: Delta State is strong in oil palm, cassava, plantain, rubber, and rice in its riverine areas, with a wet tropical rainforest climate.

q: What crops grow in Ogun State?
a: Ogun State grows cassava, maize, cocoa, oil palm, pineapple, and vegetables, and is close to Lagos markets which makes it strong for perishable produce like tomato and pepper too.

q: When should I plant maize in Kano?
a: Plant maize in Kano in March-April for the first season, or July-August for the second season. Wait until the soil has good moisture from the first rains before planting.

q: When should I plant rice in Kebbi?
a: Plant rice in Kebbi in May-July for rain-fed rice, or November-February if you have irrigation. Kebbi's irrigation systems allow for dry-season rice as well as the main rainy season crop.

q: When should I plant tomato?
a: Tomato is best planted October-February in the dry season under irrigation (highest market prices), or May-June in the rainy season for a lower-cost crop with more disease pressure.

q: When is the best time to plant cassava?
a: Plant cassava at the start of the rains in March-May for the main season, or October-November for a minor season in areas with reliable rainfall.

q: When should I plant yam?
a: Plant yam early, in February-March, for the best yield. Late planting in April is possible but generally gives a smaller harvest.

q: When should I plant groundnut?
a: Plant groundnut in May-July at the start of the rainy season. Wait for gypsum application at flowering to support proper pod (peg) formation.

q: When should I plant onion?
a: Plant onion in October-November, right after the rains end, using irrigation to carry the crop through the dry season.

q: When is the rainy season in northern Nigeria?
a: In northern Nigeria (Sudan Savanna and Sahel zones), the rainy season runs roughly May to October, though the far north (Sahel) may only get rain June to September.

q: When is the rainy season in southern Nigeria?
a: In southern Nigeria (rainforest and derived savanna zones), rain falls almost year-round, with two peaks: March-July and September-November, and only a short dry spell December-February.

q: What is harmattan?
a: Harmattan is the dry, dusty, cool wind that blows from the Sahara Desert across Nigeria, typically from November to February, most intense in the north and mild in the south.

q: How do I fix acidic soil?
a: Apply agricultural lime at 1-4 tonnes per hectare depending on how acidic the soil is, then wait 4-6 weeks before planting. Adding organic compost also helps buffer soil pH over time.

q: What soil is best for maize?
a: Maize grows best in well-drained loamy soil with a pH of 5.5-7.0. Avoid heavy waterlogged clay or very poor sandy soils without added organic matter.

q: What soil is best for cassava?
a: Cassava does well in sandy loam soil with a pH of 5.5-6.5, and tolerates poorer soils better than most staple crops, which is why it is common in the derived savanna and forest zones.

q: How do I test my soil?
a: You can test soil at NAERLS offices, state Ministry of Agriculture labs, or nearby universities for around 3,000-8,000 naira per sample. This tells you pH, NPK levels, and organic matter content.

q: What fertilizer should I use for maize?
a: Use NPK 15-15-15 at planting, then top-dress with urea about 6 weeks later when plants are knee-high, at roughly 150kg per hectare.

q: How do I improve poor sandy soil?
a: Add 5-10 tonnes of compost or organic matter per hectare, apply fertilizer in small split doses to reduce leaching, and mulch the surface to reduce evaporation and retain moisture.

q: What is the current price of maize?
a: Maize prices vary by season: N18,000-25,000 per 100kg bag right after harvest (October-December), rising to N35,000-55,000 in the lean season (April-August). Always check current prices at your local market before selling.

q: When is the best time to sell cassava?
a: The best time to sell cassava, or its processed products like garri, is December-March when prices for processed cassava products peak. Selling fresh cassava raw earns far less than processing it first.

q: Where can I sell my maize?
a: Dawanau International Market in Kano is the largest grain market in West Africa for maize. Mile 12 Market in Lagos and Gboko Market in Benue are also strong options depending on your location.

q: How do I add value to my crops before selling?
a: Process raw crops into higher-value products: cassava into garri or flour, tomato into paste or powder, groundnut into oil, and maize into flour. Processing can double or triple your profit compared to selling raw.

q: How should I store my grain after harvest?
a: Use hermetic (airtight) storage bags to protect grain from weevils and moisture. Dry grain to about 12-13% moisture before storage, and store in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight.

q: What crops give the best ROI for investors?
a:
The highest ROI crops for Nigerian agriculture are:
- Ginger (Very High ROI, 10/10 export demand)
- Sesame (Very High ROI, 10/10 export demand)
- Cocoa (Very High ROI, 10/10 export demand)
- Cashew (Very High ROI, 10/10 export demand)
- Hibiscus/Zobo (High ROI, 9/10 export demand)
- Strawberry (Very High ROI, but restricted to Plateau highlands)

q: Which states are best for agricultural investment?
a:
Top states depend on the crop:
- Kaduna, Nasarawa, Plateau for ginger
- Ondo, Cross River, Ogun for cocoa
- Kogi, Kwara, Oyo for cashew
- Benue, Nasarawa for sesame
- Kebbi, Niger for rice production and milling

q: What are the export opportunities in Nigerian agriculture?
a: Nigeria's top agricultural exports are sesame, cashew, cocoa, ginger, and hibiscus. These crops all score 9-10 out of 10 on export demand due to strong international market demand and relatively limited local processing competition.

q: How do foreign investors get land for farming in Nigeria?
a: Foreign investors generally cannot directly own land under the Land Use Act. Land is typically accessed through long-term leases (often 25-99 years) via state governments, or through joint venture partnerships with a registered Nigerian agribusiness.

q: Can foreign investors repatriate profits from Nigerian agribusiness?
a: Yes, profit repatriation is permitted through the Certificate of Capital Importation (CCI) process via the Central Bank of Nigeria, provided investment funds were originally brought in through official banking channels.

q: What financing is available for local farmers?
a: Key programs include the CBN Anchor Borrowers Programme, NIRSAL loan guarantees, Bank of Agriculture loans, and AFEX Commodities Exchange for structured trade finance and warehousing.

q: What is the investment size for rice milling in Nigeria?
a: Rice production and milling investment ranges from N50 million for a small integrated farm to N5 billion for a large-scale operation, with an ROI timeline of 3-5 years and estimated returns of 25-40% IRR.

q: What is the investment opportunity in ginger?
a: Ginger has the highest export demand score (10/10) of any Nigerian crop, with profit potential of N2,000,000 to N5,000,000 per hectare and Very High ROI classification. Best states are Kaduna, Nasarawa, and Plateau.

q: What is the investment opportunity in cocoa?
a: Cocoa offers Very High ROI with profit of N800,000 to N2,500,000 per hectare, though it takes 3-5 years to first harvest. Nigeria is the world's 4th largest cocoa producer, with Ondo, Cross River, and Ogun as top states.

q: How do I control fall armyworm in maize?
a: Scout your maize field weekly for armyworm damage on leaves. Use approved insecticides like emamectin benzoate at first sign of damage, and consider early planting to avoid peak armyworm season.

q: How do I prevent cassava mosaic disease?
a: Use certified disease-free stem cuttings from a trusted source, control whiteflies which spread the virus, and remove and destroy any visibly infected plants promptly to stop it spreading.

q: How do I treat tomato blight?
a: Apply copper-based fungicide at the first sign of dark spots on leaves or fruit, ensure good spacing for airflow, and avoid overhead watering which spreads fungal spores.

q: What causes yellow leaves in maize?
a: Yellow leaves in maize are commonly caused by nitrogen deficiency (apply urea), maize streak virus (yellow streaking pattern, control leafhoppers), or waterlogging (improve drainage).

q: Yaushe zan shuka masara a Kano?
a: Shuka masara a Kano a watan Maris zuwa Afrilu don lokaci na farko, ko Yuli zuwa Agusta don lokaci na biyu. Ka tabbata kasa tana da danshi mai kyau kafin ka shuka.

q: Wane taki ya fi kyau don masara?
a: Yi amfani da NPK 15-15-15 a lokacin shuka, sannan ka kara da Urea makonni shida bayan haka lokacin da tsire-tsire suka kai tsayin gwiwa.

q: Yaushe lokacin girbi shinkafa?
a: Shinkafa na girma cikin kwanaki 100 zuwa 130 bayan shuka. Idan an shuka a watan Mayu, girbi zai fara a watan Satumba.

q: Ta yaya zan inganta kasa mai yashi?
a: Kara da takin gargajiya (kwampost) kilogiram 5,000 zuwa 10,000 a kowace hekta, ka yi amfani da taki kadan kadan don rage asara, kuma ka rufe kasa da ciyawa don rage bushewa.

q: Menene farashin masara a yanzu?
a: Farashin masara ya bambanta da lokaci: bayan girbi (Oktoba-Disamba) yakan kasance naira 18,000-25,000 a jaka mai kilogiram 100, amma a lokacin karanci (Afrilu-Agusta) zai iya kai naira 35,000-55,000.

q: Wadanne amfanin gona suka fi samun riba ga masu zuba jari?
a: Amfanin gona masu ba da riba mafi girma su ne: Ginger (riba mai girma sosai), Sesame (riba mai girma sosai), Koko (riba mai girma sosai), da Cashew (riba mai girma sosai). Duk wadannan suna da bukatar fitarwa mai yawa zuwa kasashen waje.
